Contents: %r>\" % (self.name, self.nodelist)\n    50→\n    51→    def render(self, context):\n    52→        block_context = context.render_context.get(BLOCK_CONTEXT_KEY)\n    53→        with context.push():\n    54→            if block_context is None:\n    55→                context[\"block\"] = self\n    56→                result = self.nodelist.render(context)\n    57→            else:\n    58→                push = block = block_context.pop(self.name)\n    59→                if block is None:\n    60→                    block = self\n    61→                # Create new block so we can store context without\n    62→                # thread-safety issues.\n    63→                block = type(self)(block.name, block.nodelist)\n    64→                block.context = context\n    65→                context[\"block\"] = block\n    66→                result = block.nodelist.render(context)\n    67→                if push is not None:\n    68→                    block_context.push(self.name, push)\n    69→        return result\n    70→\n    71→    def super(self):\n    72→        if not hasattr(self, \"context\"):\n    73→            raise TemplateSyntaxError(\n    74→                \"'%s' object has no attribute 'context'. A history is kept in\n   102→        the render_context attribute between successive extends calls and\n   103→        passed as the skip argument. This enables extends to work recursively\n   104→        without extending the same template twice.\n   105→        \"\"\"\n   106→        history = context.render_context.setdefault(\n   107→            self.context_key,\n   108→            [self.origin],\n   109→        )\n   110→        template, origin = context.template.engine.find_template(\n   111→            template_name,\n   112→            skip=history,\n   113→        )\n   114→        history.append(origin)\n   115→        return template\n   116→\n   117→    def get_parent(self, context):\n   118→        parent = self.parent_name.resolve(context)\n   119→        if not parent:\n   120→            error_msg = \"Invalid template name in 'extends' tag: %r.\" % parent\n   121→            if self.parent_name.filters or isinstance(self.parent_name.var, Variable):\n   122→                error_msg += (\n   123→                    \" Got this from the '%s' variable.\" % self.parent_name.token\n   124→                )\n   125→            raise TemplateSyntaxError(error_msg)\n   126→        if isinstance(parent, Template):\n   127→            # Add the block nodes from this node to the block context\n   142→        block_context.add_blocks(self.blocks)\n   143→\n   144→        # If this block's parent doesn't have an extends node it is the root,\n   145→        # and its block nodes also need to be added to the block context.\n   146→        for node in compiled_parent.nodelist:\n   147→            # The ExtendsNode has to be the first non-text node.\n   148→            if not isinstance(node, TextNode):\n   149→                if not isinstance(node, ExtendsNode):\n   150→                    blocks = {\n   151→                        n.name: n\n   152→                        for n in compiled_parent.nodelist.get_nodes_by_type(BlockNode)\n   153→                    }\n   154→                    block_context.add_blocks(blocks)\n   155→                break\n   156→\n   157→        # Call Template._render explicitly so the parser context stays\n   158→        # the same.\n   159→        with context.render_context.push_state(compiled_parent, isolated_context=False):\n   160→            return compiled_parent._render(context)\n   161→\n   162→\n   163→class IncludeNode(Node):\n   164→    context_key = \"__include_context\"\n   165→\n   166→    def __init__(\n   167→        self, template, *args, extra_context=None, isolated_context=False, **kwargs\n   168→    ):\n   169→        self.template = template\n   170→        self.extra_context = extra_context or {}\n   171→        self.isolated_context = isolated_context\n   172→        super().__init__(*args, **kwargs)\n   173→\n   174→    def __repr__(self):\n   175→        return f\"<{self.__class__.__qualname__}: template={self.template!r}>\"\n   176→\n   177→    def render(self, context):\n   178→        \"\"\"\n   179→        Render the specified template and context. Cache the template object\n   180→        in render_context to avoid reparsing and loading when used in a for\n   181→        loop.\n   182→        \"\"\"\n   183→        template = self.template.resolve(context)\n   184→        # Use the base.Template of a backends.django.Template.\n   203→        elif hasattr(template, \"template\"):\n   204→            template = template.template\n   205→        values = {\n   206→            name: var.resolve(context) for name, var in self.extra_context.items()\n   207→        }\n   208→        if self.isolated_context:\n   209→            return template.render(context.new(values))\n   210→        with context.push(**values):\n   211→            return template.render(context)\n   212→\n   213→\n   214→@register.tag(\"block\")\n   215→def do_block(parser, token):\n   216→    \"\"\"\n   217→    Define a block that can be overridden by child templates.\n   218→    \"\"\"\n   219→    # token.split_contents() isn't useful here because this tag doesn't accept\n   220→    # variable as arguments.\n   221→    bits = token.contents.split()\n   222→    if len(bits) != 2:\n   223→        raise TemplateSyntaxError(\"'%s' tag takes only one argument\" % bits[0])\n   224→    block_name = bits[1]\n   225→    # Keep track of the names of BlockNodes found in this template, so we can\n   226→    # check for duplication.\n   227→    try:\n   228→        if block_name in parser.__loaded_blocks:\n   229→            raise TemplateSyntaxError(\n   230→                \"'%s' tag with name '%s' appears more than once\" % (bits[0], block_name)\n   231→            )\n   232→        parser.__loaded_blocks.append(block_name)\n   233→    except AttributeError:  # parser.__loaded_blocks isn't a list yet\n   234→        parser.__loaded_blocks = [block_name]\n   235→    nodelist = parser.parse((\"endblock\",))\n   236→\n   237→    # This check is kept for backwards-compatibility. See #3100.\n   238→    endblock = parser.next_token()\n   239→    acceptable_endblocks = (\"endblock\", \"endblock %s\" % block_name)\n   240→    if endblock.contents not in acceptable_endblocks:\n   241→        parser.invalid_block_tag(endblock, \"endblock\", acceptable_endblocks)\n   242→\n   243→    return BlockNode(block_name, nodelist)\n   244→\n   245→\n   246→def construct_relative_path(\n   247→    current_template_name,\n   248→    relative_name,\n   249→    allow_recursion=False,\n   250→):\n   251→    \"\"\"\n   252→    Convert a relative path (starting with './' or '../') to the full template\n   253→    name based on the current_template_name.\n   254→    \"\"\"\n   255→    new_name = relative_name.strip(\"'\\\"\")\n   256→    if not new_name.startswith((\"./\", \"../\")):\n   257→        # relative_name is a variable or a literal that doesn't contain a\n   258→        # relative path.\n   259→        return relative_name\n   260→\n   261→    if current_template_name is None:\n   262→        # Unknown origin (e.g. Template('...').render(Context({...})).\n   263→        raise TemplateSyntaxError(\n   264→            f\"The relative path {relative_name} cannot be evaluated due to \"\n   265→            \"an unknown template origin.\"\n   266→        )\n   267→\n   268→    new_name = posixpath.normpath(\n   269→        posixpath.join(\n   270→            posixpath.dirname(current_template_name.lstrip(\"/\")),\n   271→            new_name,\n   272→        )\n   273→    )\n   274→    if new_name.startswith(\"../\"):\n   275→        raise TemplateSyntaxError(\n   276→            \"The relative path '%s' points outside the file hierarchy that \"\n   277→            \"template '%s' is in.\" % (relative_name, current_template_name)\n   278→        )\n   279→    if not allow_recursion and current_template_name.lstrip(\"/\") == new_name:\n   280→        raise TemplateSyntaxError(\n   281→            \"The relative path '%s' was translated to template name '%s', the \"\n   282→            \"same template in which the tag appears.\"\n   283→            % (relative_name, current_template_name)\n   284→        )\n   285→    has_quotes = (\n   286→        relative_name.startswith(('\"', \"'\")) and relative_name[0] == relative_name[-1]\n   287→    )\n   288→    return f'\"{new_name}\"' if has_quotes else new_name\n   289→\n   290→\n   291→@register.tag(\"extends\")\n   292→def do_extends(parser, token):\n   293→    \"\"\"\n   294→    Signal that this template extends a parent template.\n   295→\n   296→    This tag may be used in two ways: ``{% extends \"base\" %}`` (with quotes)\n   297→    uses the literal value \"base\" as the name of the parent template to extend,\n   298→    or ``{% extends variable %}`` uses the value of ``variable`` as either the\n   299→    name of the parent template to extend (if it evaluates to a string) or as\n   300→    the parent template itself (if it evaluates to a Template object).\n   301→    \"\"\"\n   302→    bits = token.split_contents()\n   303→    if len(bits) != 2:\n   304→        raise TemplateSyntaxError(\"'%s' takes one argument\" % bits[0])\n   305→    bits[1] = construct_relative_path(parser.origin.template_name, bits[1])\n   306→    parent_name = parser.compile_filter(bits[1])\n   307→    nodelist = parser.parse()\n   308→    if nodelist.get_nodes_by_type(ExtendsNode):\n   309→        raise TemplateSyntaxError(\n   310→            \"'%s' cannot appear more than once in the same template\" % bits[0]\n   311→        )\n   312→    return ExtendsNode(nodelist, parent_name)\n   313→\n   314→\n   315→@register.tag(\"include\")\n   316→def do_include(parser, token):\n   317→    \"\"\"\n   318→    Load a template and render it with the current context. You can pass\n   319→    additional context using keyword arguments.\n   320→\n   321→    Example::\n   322→\n   323→        {% include \"foo/some_include\" %}\n   324→        {% include \"foo/some_include\" with bar=\"BAZZ!\" baz=\"BING!\" %}\n   325→\n   326→    Use the ``only`` argument to exclude the current context when rendering\n   327→    the included template::\n   328→\n   329→        {% include \"foo/some_include\" only %}\n   330→        {% include \"foo/some_include\" with bar=\"1\" only %}\n   331→    \"\"\"\n   332→    bits = token.split_contents()\n   333→    if 337→        )\n   338→    options = {}\n   339→    remaining_bits = bits[2:]\n   340→    while remaining_bits:\n   341→        option = remaining_bits.pop(0)\n   342→        if option in options:\n   343→            raise TemplateSyntaxError(\n   344→                \"The %r option was specified more than once.\" % option\n   345→            )\n   346→        if option == \"with\":\n   347→            value = token_kwargs(remaining_bits, parser, support_legacy=False)\n   348→            if not value:\n   349→                raise TemplateSyntaxError(\n   350→                    '\"with\" in %r 364→        allow_recursion=True,\n   365→    )\n   366→    return IncludeNode(\n   367→        parser.compile_filter(bits[1]),\n   368→        extra_context=namemap,\n   369→ Contents: %r>\" % (self.name, self.nodelist)\n\n    def render(self, context):\n        block_context = context.render_context.get(BLOCK_CONTEXT_KEY)\n        with context.push():\n            if block_context is None:\n                context[\"block\"] = self\n                result = self.nodelist.render(context)\n            else:\n                push = block = block_context.pop(self.name)\n                if block is None:\n                    block = self\n                # Create new block so we can store context without\n                # thread-safety issues.\n                block = type(self)(block.name, block.nodelist)\n                block.context = context\n                context[\"block\"] = block\n                result = block.nodelist.render(context)\n                if push is not None:\n                    block_context.push(self.name, push)\n        return result\n\n    def super(self):\n        if not hasattr(self, \"context\"):\n            raise TemplateSyntaxError(\n                \"'%s' object has no attribute 'context'. A history is kept in\n        the render_context attribute between successive extends calls and\n        passed as the skip argument. Call Template._render explicitly so the parser context stays\n        # the same.\n        with context.render_context.push_state(compiled_parent, isolated_context=False):\n            return compiled_parent._render(context)\n\n\nclass IncludeNode(Node):\n    context_key = \"__include_context\"\n\n    def __init__(\n        self, template, *args, extra_context=None, isolated_context=False, **kwargs\n    ):\n        self.template = template\n        self.extra_context = extra_context or {}\n        self.isolated_context = isolated_context\n        super().__init__(*args, **kwargs)\n\n    def __repr__(self):\n        return f\"<{self.__class__.__qualname__}: template={self.template!r}>\"\n\n    def render(self, context):\n        \"\"\"\n        Render the specified template and context. Cache the template object\n        in render_context to avoid reparsing and loading when used in a for\n        loop.\n        \"\"\"\n        template = self.template.resolve(context)\n        # Does this quack like a Template?\n        if not callable(getattr(template, \"render\", None)):\n            #    allow_recursion=False,\n):\n    \"\"\"\n    Convert a relative path (starting with './' or '../') to the full template\n    name based on the current_template_name.\n    \"\"\"\n    new_name = relative_name.strip(\"'\\\"\")\n    if not new_name.startswith((\"./\", \"../\")):\n        # relative_name is a variable or a literal that doesn't contain a\n        # relative path.\n        return relative_name\n\n    if current_template_name is None:\n        # Unknown origin (e.g. Template('...').render(Context({...})).\n        raise TemplateSyntaxError(\n            f\"The relative path path '%s' points outside the file hierarchy that \"\n            \"template '%s' is in.\" % (relative_name, current_template_name)\n        )\n    if not allow_recursion and current_template_name.lstrip(\"/\") == new_name:\n        raise TemplateSyntaxError(\n            \"The relative path '%s' was translated to template name '%s', the \"\n            \"same template in which the tag appears.\"\n            % (relative_name, current_template_name)\n        )\n    has_quotes = (\n        relative_name.startswith(('\"', \"'\")) and relative_name[0] == relative_name[-1]\n    )\n    return f'\"{new_name}\"' if has_quotes else new_name\n\n\n@register.tag(\"extends\")\ndef do_extends(parser, token):\n    \"\"\"\n    Signal that this template extends a parent template.\n\n    This tag may be used in two ways: ``{% extends \"base\" %}`` (with quotes)\n    uses the literal value \"base\" as the name of the parent template to extend,\n    or ``{% extends variable %}`` uses the value of ``variable`` as either the\n    name of the parent template to extend (if it evaluates to a string) or as\n    the parent template itself (if it evaluates to a {"type":"assistant","message":{"model":"claude-haiku-4-5-20251001","id":"msg_015URrdbPhf4JD6urj3TyN6N","type":"message","role":"assistant","content":[{"type":"thinking","thinking":"Let me analyze the block inheritance mechanism. Looking at the code:\n\n1. `BlockNode` - represents a block that can be overridden\n   - `render()` method: Uses block_context to pop/push blocks\n   \n2. `ExtendsNode` - represents template inheritance\n   - Line 94: Gets all BlockNode instances from its nodelist (the child template)\n   - Lines 141-142: Adds those blocks to the block_context via `block_context.add_blocks(self.blocks)`\n   - Lines 146-155: If parent doesn't have ExtendsNode, adds parent's blocks to block_context\n\n3. `BlockContext` - manages a FIFO queue of blocks\n   - `add_blocks()` - inserts blocks at position 0 (beginning)\n   - `pop()` - pops from the end\n   - `get_block()` - gets the last block\n   - `push()` - appends to end\n\nThe logic seems to be:\n- When rendering a child template, its blocks are added to the BlockContext\n- When a BlockNode renders, it pops from the BlockContext to get the override\n- Then it pushes back to maintain state\n\nLet me trace through what should happen with 3 levels:\n\nSay we have:\n- base.html with `{% block outer %}base outer{% block inner %}base inner{% endblock %}{% endblock %}`\n- layout.html extends base.html with `{% block outer %}layout outer{% block inner %}layout inner{% endblock %}{% endblock %}`\n- page.html extends layout.html with `{% block inner %}page inner{% endblock %}`\n\nThe issue described is: \"when the innermost template overrides a block that is nested inside another overridden block, the content from the middle template leaks through\"\n\nSo when rendering page.html:\n1. ExtendsNode for page.html processes\n   - blocks = {inner: page's BlockNode for inner}\n   - block_context.add_blocks({inner: page's BlockNode})\n   \n2. Then layout.html renders as the parent\n   - Its ExtendsNode collects {outer: layout's BlockNode, inner: layout's BlockNode}\n   - These get added to block_context\n   \n3. Then base.html renders as the final parent\n   - No ExtendsNode, so base's blocks {outer: base's BlockNode, inner: base's BlockNode} are added to block_context\n\n4. Now when base.html renders, the block_context queue has all these blocks stacked up\n\n. Each block name maps to a FIFO queue where blocks are inserted at the beginning when `add_blocks()` is called. So outer contains [base's outer, layout's outer] and inner contains [base's inner, layout's inner, page's inner]. The `pop()` method removes from the end of the queue, `push()` appends to the end, and `get_block()` retrieves the appropriate block for rendering.\n\nWhen ExtendsNode processes templates in order (child to parent), it inserts each template's blocks at position 0, building up the queue. Then during BlockNode rendering, `pop()` retrieves blocks from the end of the queue, which gives us the innermost (most specific) block first.\n\nThe real complexity emerges with nested blocks. In base.html, the outer block's nodelist contains both text content and a nested inner BlockNode. When layout.html extends base.html and defines its own outer block, that block's nodelist replaces the base's outer content entirely. The inner BlockNode that was nested inside base's outer block is now lost unless layout.html also defines an inner block. This is where the inheritance chain matters—each level can override parent blocks, but nested blocks within those overridden blocks get replaced unless explicitly preserved through the child template's block definitions. nodelist contains layout's inner BlockNode.\n\nWhen rendering layout's inner BlockNode:\n- pop(inner) gets page's inner block\n- This is correct, we want page's inner\n\nBut I should verify this logic against actual test cases to understand where the problem might be occurring.","signature":""}],"stop_reason":null,"stop_sequence":null,"usage":{"input_tokens":12,"cache_creation_input_tokens":5023,"cache_read_input_tokens":21899,"cache_creation":{"ephemeral_5m_input_tokens":0,"ephemeral_1h_input_tokens":5023},"output_tokens":5,"service_tier":"standard","inference_geo":"not_available"},"context_management":null},"parent_tool_use_id":null,"session_id":"9289f73d-547f-4b33-bcf6-616cce479223","uuid":"565ee87e-50c3-4603-ab18-bec77430d6d5"}

Let me just read through the code and understand the logic analytically instead. Let me create a more detailed trace of what should happen.\n\nLooking back at the loader_tags.py code, I need to understand how the block inheritance works at 3 levels.\n\nThe key insight from the bug report is:\n- \"when a template uses three or more levels of inheritance (e.g., `base.html` -> `layout.html` -> `page.html`), and the innermost template overrides a block that is nested inside another overridden block, the content from the middle template \"leaks through\"\"\n\nSo the problem is specifically about NESTED blocks being overridden. Let me trace through the logic more carefully.\n\nLet's say:\n- base.html: `{% block outer %}base-outer[{% block inner %}base-inner{% endblock %}]{% endblock %}`\n- layout.html: `{% extends \"base.html\" %}{% block outer %}layout-outer[{% block inner %}layout-inner{% endblock %}]{% endblock %}`\n- page.html: `{% extends \"layout.html\" %}{% block inner %}page-inner{% endblock %}`\n\nWhen rendering page.html, here's what should happen:\n1. page.html's template is loaded\n2. It has an ExtendsNode that references layout.html\n3. ExtendsNode.render() is called:\n   - self.blocks = {inner: page.html's inner BlockNode}\n   - block_context.add_blocks({inner: page.html's inner})\n   - rendered parent is layout.html\n   - layout.html also has an ExtendsNode\n   - Actually, the ExtendsNode renders the parent template, so...\n\nActually, I realize I need to understand the Template structure better. When you load a template that has `{% extends %}`, the entire parsed template (including the ExtendsNode) is stored. Then when you render it, the ExtendsNode.render() method is called first (since it's in the nodelist).\n\n\nThe ExtendsNode must be first, so when Template renders, it processes this node immediately. Looking at ExtendsNode.render(), it retrieves the parent template and initializes a BlockContext if needed, then adds the current template's blocks to that context before rendering the parent. with context.render_context.push_state(compiled_parent, isolated_context=False):\n        return compiled_parent._render(context)\n\nThe process chains together: first, it adds the current template's block overrides to the context, then checks whether the parent template itself extends another template. If the parent is a root template (no ExtendsNode), it also adds the parent's blocks. Finally, it renders the parent while maintaining the parser context through push_state().\n\nWhen page.html renders, its ExtendsNode retrieves layout.html as the parent, registers page.html's block overrides like {inner: page-inner}, and since layout.html also has an ExtendsNode, the chain continues up the inheritance hierarchy.\n\nDuring layout.html's render phase, its ExtendsNode finds base.html as the parent and merges blocks accordingly—layout.html's blocks get added first, then page.html's overrides are inserted at the front of the inner block, creating the sequence [layout-inner, page-inner]. Since base.html has no ExtendsNode, its blocks are also merged in, resulting in the final block stack [base-outer[...], layout-outer[...]] for outer and [base-inner, layout-inner, page-inner] for inner.\n\nWhen base.html finally renders, it encounters BlockNode(\"outer\") and calls its render method to output the accumulated block content from the inheritance chain. returns the last item.\n\nSo if blocks[inner] = [base-inner, layout-inner, page-inner], pop() removes and returns page-inner, leaving [base-inner, layout-inner] behind.\n\nNow looking at add_blocks more carefully - it uses insert(0, block) to add at the beginning, shifting existing items right. This means successive calls build the list in reverse order: calling add_blocks three times with page-inner, then layout-inner, then base-inner results in [base-inner, layout-inner, page-inner]. When we pop from the end, we get page-inner first, which is the most recently added block.\n\nThe issue becomes clearer when considering the actual node structure. Each template file creates its own BlockNode objects - base.html's BlockNode(\"outer\") is a separate object from layout.html's BlockNode(\"outer\"), even though they have the same name. When extracting blocks from different template levels, we're working with distinct node instances that each contain their own nested block definitions.\n\nThe blocks dictionary gets populated with these different instances organized by name: blocks[outer] contains the outer blocks from both base and layout templates, while blocks[inner] contains inner blocks from all three templates. During rendering, when we encounter a block node and pop from the corresponding stack, we're retrieving the override from the child template. But there's a potential issue with how nested blocks interact - when we render a parent block's nodelist that contains child blocks, we need to ensure we're popping from the correct stack at the right time, especially when multiple levels of nesting are involved. [outer] = [layout.html's outer]\nAfter call 3: blocks[inner] = [base.html's inner, layout.html's inner, page.html's inner], blocks[outer] = [base.html's outer, layout.html's outer]\n\nWhen rendering, pop() retrieves the last item, which should be correct. But wait—maybe the problem occurs when the innermost template only overrides a nested block without overriding its parent block. In that scenario, the parent block from the middle template gets rendered, but it might reference the nested block incorrectly since the stack order could be misaligned.\n\nLet me trace through what happens if page.html only defines the inner block without redefining outer. The extracted blocks would be {inner: page.html's BlockNode}, but this inner block exists at the top level of page.html, not nested within an outer block. When rendering base.html's outer block (which contains inner), and then rendering the inner block within it, popping from blocks[inner] gives page.html's inner. However, the issue might be that base.html's outer block structure isn't being properly preserved when the leaf template skips redefining it.\n\nLooking at the BlockNode.render() method, when block_context exists, it pops the block by name and creates a new BlockNode instance with the popped block's name and nodelist. This means the nodelist being rendered comes from whichever block was pushed onto the stack—in this case, page.html's inner block containing \"page-inner\". The render logic seems to handle this correctly by using the popped block's nodelist rather than the original block's nodelist.\n\nBut examining the push/pop mechanism more closely, there's a potential issue with how blocks are restored. The code pops a block by name, renders it, then pushes it back only if the popped value wasn't None. Since `push` gets assigned the result of `pop()`, if nothing was pushed for that block name, `pop()` returns None and the block never gets restored to the stack.\n\nLet me trace through a concrete example with inheritance chains. If base.html defines outer and inner blocks, layout.html extends base and overrides both, and page.html extends layout but only overrides inner, the rendering order matters. When rendering page.html's inner block, the code pops from the context stack—but if the stack state isn't managed correctly across the inheritance chain, it might grab the wrong block or leave blocks in an inconsistent state for subsequent renders.\n\nThe core issue seems to be in how blocks are extracted and tracked during parsing. When a template with nested blocks is parsed, the parser creates a nodelist containing only direct children, but nested blocks are still accessible through `get_nodes_by_type()`. During ExtendsNode initialization, blocks are extracted into a dictionary, which should capture all BlockNodes regardless of nesting depth. However, if the extraction or the subsequent popping mechanism doesn't account for the full inheritance hierarchy, content from an intermediate template could leak through instead of being properly overridden.\n\nWhen rendering the base template, each block node pops from its corresponding block stack. If page.html's inner block isn't properly placed at the top of the stack, or if the stack order gets corrupted during the add_blocks process, the wrong block content could be rendered. Does this quack like a Template?\n+        if not callable(getattr(template, \"render\", None)):\n+            # If not, try the cache and select_template().\n+            template_name = template or ()\n+            if isinstance(template_name, str):\n+                template_name = (\n+                    construct_relative_path(\n+                        self.origin.template_name,\n+                        template_name,\n+                    ),\n+                )\n+            else:\n+                template_name = tuple(template_name)\n+            cache = context.render_context.dicts[0].setdefault(self, {})\n+            template = cache.get(template_name)\n+            if template is None:\n+                template = context.template.engine.select_template(template_name)\n+                cache[template_name] = template\n+        # Use the base.Template of a backends.django.Template.\n+        elif hasattr(template, \"template\"):\n+            template = template.template\n+        values = {\n+            name: var.resolve(context) for name, var in self.extra_context.items()\n+        }\n+        if self.isolated_context:\n+            return template.render(context.new(values))\n+        with context.push(**values):\n+            return template.render(context)\n+\n+\n+@register.tag(\"block\")\n+def do_block(parser, token):\n+    \"\"\"\n+    Define a block that can be overridden by child templates.\n+    \"\"\"\n+    # token.split_contents() isn't useful here because this tag doesn't accept\n+    # variable as arguments.\n+    bits = token.contents.split()\n+    if len(bits) != 2:\n+        raise TemplateSyntaxError(\"'%s' tag takes only one argument\" % bits[0])\n+    block_name = bits[1]\n+    # Keep track of the names of BlockNodes found in this template, so we can\n+    # check for duplication.\n+    try:\n+        if block_name in parser.__loaded_blocks:\n+            raise TemplateSyntaxError(\n+                \"'%s' tag with name '%s' appears more than once\" % (bits[0], block_name)\n+            )\n+        parser.__loaded_blocks.append(block_name)\n+    except AttributeError:  # parser.__loaded_blocks isn't a list yet\n+        parser.__loaded_blocks = [block_name]\n+    nodelist = parser.parse((\"endblock\",))\n+\n+    # This check is kept for backwards-compatibility. See #3100.\n+    endblock = parser.next_token()\n+    acceptable_endblocks = (\"endblock\", \"endblock %s\" % block_name)\n+    if endblock.contents not in acceptable_endblocks:\n+        parser.invalid_block_tag(endblock, \"endblock\", acceptable_endblocks)\n+\n+    return BlockNode(block_name, nodelist)\n+\n+\n+def construct_relative_path(\n+    current_template_name,\n+    relative_name,\n+    allow_recursion=False,\n+):\n+    \"\"\"\n+    Convert a relative path (starting with './' or '../') to the full template\n+    name based on the current_template_name.\n+    \"\"\"\n+    new_name = relative_name.strip(\"'\\\"\")\n+    if not new_name.startswith((\"./\", \"../\")):\n+        # relative_name is a variable or a literal that doesn't contain a\n+        # relative path.\n+        return relative_name\n+\n+    if current_template_name is None:\n+        # Unknown origin (e.g. Template('...').render(Context({...})).\n+        raise TemplateSyntaxError(\n+            f\"The relative path {relative_name} cannot be evaluated due to \"\n+            \"an unknown template origin.\"\n+        )\n+\n+    new_name = posixpath.normpath(\n+        posixpath.join(\n+            posixpath.dirname(current_template_name.lstrip(\"/\")),\n+            new_name,\n+        )\n+    )\n+    if new_name.startswith(\"../\"):\n+        raise TemplateSyntaxError(\n+            \"The relative path '%s' points outside the file hierarchy that \"\n+            \"template '%s' is in.\" % (relative_name, current_template_name)\n+        )\n+    if not allow_recursion and current_template_name.lstrip(\"/\") == new_name:\n+        raise TemplateSyntaxError(\n+            \"The relative path '%s' was translated to template name '%s', the \"\n+            \"same template in which the tag appears.\"\n+            % (relative_name, current_template_name)\n+        )\n+    has_quotes = (\n+        relative_name.startswith(('\"', \"'\")) and relative_name[0] == relative_name[-1]\n+    )\n+    return f'\"{new_name}\"' if has_quotes else new_name\n+\n+\n+@register.tag(\"extends\")\n+def do_extends(parser, token):\n+    \"\"\"\n+    Signal that this template extends a parent template.\n+\n+    This tag may be used in two ways: ``{% extends \"base\" %}`` (with quotes)\n+    uses the literal value \"base\" as the name of the parent template to extend,\n+    or ``{% extends variable %}`` uses the value of ``variable`` as either the\n+    name of the parent template to extend (if it evaluates to a string) or as\n+    the parent template itself (if it evaluates to a Template object).\n+    \"\"\"\n+    bits = token.split_contents()\n+    if len(bits) != 2:\n+        raise TemplateSyntaxError(\"'%s' takes one argument\" % bits[0])\n+    bits[1] = construct_relative_path(parser.origin.template_name, bits[1])\n+    parent_name = parser.compile_filter(bits[1])\n+    nodelist = parser.parse()\n+    if nodelist.get_nodes_by_type(ExtendsNode):\n+        raise TemplateSyntaxError(\n+            \"'%s' cannot appear more than once in the same template\" % bits[0]\n+        )\n+    return ExtendsNode(nodelist, parent_name)\n+\n+\n+@register.tag(\"include\")\n+def do_include(parser, token):\n+    \"\"\"\n+    Load a template and render it with the current context. You can pass\n+    additional context using keyword arguments.\n+\n+    Example::\n+\n+        {% include \"foo/some_include\" %}\n+        {% include \"foo/some_include\" with bar=\"BAZZ!\" baz=\"BING!\" %}\n+\n+    Use the ``only`` argument to exclude the current context when rendering\n+    the included template::\n+\n+        {% include \"foo/some_include\" only %}\n+        {% include \"foo/some_include\" with bar=\"1\" only %}\n+    \"\"\"\n+    bits = token.split_contents()\n+    if len(bits) < 2:\n+        raise TemplateSyntaxError(\n+            \"%r tag takes at least one argument: the name of the template to \"\n+            \"be included.\" % bits[0]\n+        )\n+    options = {}\n+    remaining_bits = bits[2:]\n+    while remaining_bits:\n+        option = remaining_bits.pop(0)\n+        if option in options:\n+            raise TemplateSyntaxError(\n+                \"The %r option was specified more than once.\" % option\n+            )\n+        if option == \"with\":\n+            value = token_kwargs(remaining_bits, parser, support_legacy=False)\n+            if not value:\n+                raise TemplateSyntaxError(\n+                    '\"with\" in %r tag needs at least one keyword argument.' % bits[0]\n+                )\n+        elif option == \"only\":\n+            value = True\n+        else:\n+            raise TemplateSyntaxError(\n+                \"Unknown argument for %r tag: %r.\" % (bits[0], option)\n+            )\n+        options[option] = value\n+    isolated_context = options.get(\"only\", False)\n+    namemap = options.get(\"with\", {})\n+    bits[1] = construct_relative_path(\n+        parser.origin.template_name,\n+        bits[1],\n+        allow_recursion=True,\n+    )\n+    return IncludeNode(\n+        parser.compile_filter(bits[1]),\n+        extra_context=namemap,\n+        isolated_context=isolated_context,\n+    )","stderr":"","interrupted":false,"isImage":false,"noOutputExpected":false}}

I think the issue might be with how blocks are being tracked across nested rendering contexts.\n\nWait, I think I found it! Let me look at the BlockNode.render() method again:\n\n```python\ndef render(self, context):\n    block_context = context.render_context.get(BLOCK_CONTEXT_KEY)\n    with context.push():\n        if block_context is None:\n            context[\"block\"] = self\n            result = self.nodelist.render(context)\n        else:\n            push = block = block_context.pop(self.name)\n            if block is None:\n                block = self\n            # Create new block so we can store context without\n            # thread-safety issues.\n            block = type(self)(block.name, block.nodelist)\n            block.context = context\n            context[\"block\"] = block\n            result = block.nodelist.render(context)\n            if push is not None:\n                block_context.push(self.name, push)\n    return result\n```\n\nI think the issue is subtle. When we render a block, we pop from the block_context. But if a block renders multiple blocks inside it (nested blocks), we need to make sure we're popping in the right order.\n\nLet me trace through again with a 3-level example:\n\nbase.html: 1{% block outer %}base{% block inner %}B_inner{% endblock %}{% endblock %}2\nmid.html: {% extends \"base.html\" %}{% block outer %}mid{% block inner %}M_inner{% endblock %}{% endblock %}\n\n\nleaf.html: {% extends \"mid.html\" %}{% block inner %}L_inner{% endblock %}\n\nAfter all add_blocks calls:\n- blocks[outer] = [base's outer, mid's outer]\n- blocks[inner] = [base's inner, mid's inner, leaf's inner]\n\nWhen rendering base.html's nodelist, I hit the outer BlockNode and pop mid's outer from the stack. Then I render mid's outer's nodelist which contains another BlockNode for inner. When I pop inner, I get mid's inner block, but the issue is that after rendering mid's inner, I push it back onto the stack. This means when we later encounter the base's inner block (which is nested inside base's outer), the stack still has mid's inner on top, causing it to render instead of leaf's inner.\n\nThe problem becomes clearer when I trace through the push/pop cycle: after rendering mid's outer, I restore mid's outer back to blocks[outer]. Similarly, after rendering the inner block, I push leaf's inner back. But the nesting structure means base's outer contains base's inner, so when base's inner tries to render, the blocks dictionary has already been modified by the inner block rendering that happened inside mid's outer. The restoration happens at the wrong scope level relative to the template hierarchy.","signature":""}],"stop_reason":null,"stop_sequence":null,"usage":{"input_tokens":8,"cache_creation_input_tokens":143,"cache_read_input_tokens":52992,"cache_creation":{"ephemeral_5m_input_tokens":0,"ephemeral_1h_input_tokens":143},"output_tokens":1,"service_tier":"standard","inference_geo":"not_available"},"context_management":null},"parent_tool_use_id":null,"session_id":"9289f73d-547f-4b33-bcf6-616cce479223","uuid":"e9ff1a47-b110-4515-a8ae-3a317c223874"}

{"type":"assistant","message":{"model":"claude-haiku-4-5-20251001","id":"msg_01RnnkUkpkaiVxfcztJcMEf1","type":"message","role":"assistant","content":[{"type":"thinking","thinking":"Good! So `get_nodes_by_type` recursively gets all nodes of a given type from the nodelist. This means it will correctly find nested BlockNodes.\n\nNow let me think about the issue more carefully. Looking at the ExtendsNode.__init__ (line 94 in loader_tags.py):\n\n```python\nself.blocks = {n.name: n for n in nodelist.get_nodes_by_type(BlockNode)}\n```\n\nThis gets all BlockNodes from the nodelist recursively. So if the nodelist contains:\n- BlockNode(\"outer\") with nodelist containing BlockNode(\"inner\")\n\nThen get_nodes_by_type(BlockNode) will return [BlockNode(\"outer\"), BlockNode(\"inner\")].\n\nSo the dictionary comprehension will create:\n```python\n{outer: <BlockNode outer>, inner: <BlockNode inner>}\n```\n\nBut WAIT! There's a problem! If the same block name appears at multiple levels, the dictionary comprehension will OVERWRITE the earlier one!\n\nFor example, if we have:\n- BlockNode(\"outer\") with nodelist containing BlockNode(\"inner\")And then also:\n- Another BlockNode(\"inner\") at the same level as BlockNode(\"outer\")\n\nThen both would have the same key \"inner\", and the second one would overwrite the first!\n\nLet me think about whether this could happen in the test case...mid.html: \"{% extends 'base.html' %}{% block outer %}mid{% block inner %}M_inner{% endblock %}{% endblock %}\"\n\nWhen parsing this, the parser creates:\n- BlockNode(\"outer\") with nodelist = [TextNode(\"mid\"), BlockNode(\"inner\")]\n- The inner BlockNode has nodelist = [TextNode(\"M_inner\")]\n\nThe get_nodes_by_type(BlockNode) will return:\n- BlockNode(\"outer\")\n- BlockNode(\"inner\")\n\nAnd the blocks dict will be: {outer: BlockNode(outer), inner: BlockNode(inner)}\n\nSo the inner BlockNode IS the one inside the outer BlockNode. That's correct.\n\n\nHmm, I'm still not finding the bug. Let me think about this differently. Maybe the issue is in how the blocks are being used, not how they're being extracted. Actually, wait. Let me re-read the problem statement again. It says: \"when the innermost template overrides a block that is nested inside another overridden block\". The key phrase is \"overridden block\". Let me think about what happens when the middle template overrides the outer block (and it contains an inner block), and the leaf template overrides ONLY the inner block, not the outer block. In leaf.html, we only define the inner block at the top level.\n\nWhen extracting blocks, leaf.html's inner block exists at the top level, not nested inside another block. But mid.html's inner block is nested inside its outer block, and base.html's inner block is also nested inside its outer block. So when we collect blocks[inner], we're mixing a top-level block from leaf with nested blocks from mid and base. This mismatch in nesting context could be causing the rendering issue.\n\nDuring rendering, when base.html's outer block is rendered, it pops mid's outer and renders its nodelist, which contains mid's inner BlockNode. When that inner block renders, it pops leaf's top-level inner and renders its content. After leaf's inner finishes, it gets pushed back onto the stack. Then mid's outer finishes rendering and gets pushed back. At this point, the blocks stack is restored and rendering continues with base.\n\nThe output should be base_outer_text + mid_outer_text + leaf_inner_text, which matches the expected result. Looking more closely at the BlockNode.render() implementation, I notice it pops the block from context, creates a new BlockNode instance to avoid thread-safety issues, stores the context on that block, and then renders the nodelist.\n\nWhen rendering mid's outer block, it pops mid's outer from the stack, instantiates a fresh block with the same name and nodelist, and proceeds to render that nodelist which contains the mid's inner BlockNode object itself.\n\nThen when that inner BlockNode renders, it pops leaf's inner from the stack, creates a new block instance with leaf's inner's name and nodelist, and renders that—which should correctly produce leaf's inner's content.\n\nI'm not spotting the issue yet. In ExtendsNode.render(), when processing compiled_parent.nodelist around lines 146-155, the code adds block nodes from the parent to the block context. The ExtendsNode has to be the first non-text node, and if it's not an ExtendsNode, it extracts all BlockNodes from the parent's nodelist—including nested ones—and adds them to block_context.\n\nFor base.html, this means both the outer BlockNode and the inner BlockNode (which sits inside outer) get added to block_context.blocks. When add_blocks is called with both outer and inner blocks from the base template, they both get inserted into the context at the same level.\n\nThe critical issue is that the inner BlockNode being added is the one nested inside base's outer block. So when rendering base's outer block and encountering mid's inner BlockNode (a different object), popping from blocks[inner] retrieves leaf's inner instead of respecting the nesting structure. This causes the wrong block content to be rendered in the wrong position.\n\nLooking at the actual problem: the output shows base + mid + MID_INNER when it should be base + mid + LEAF_INNER. This suggests blocks[inner] either doesn't contain leaf's inner, or they're ordered incorrectly so we're popping mid's inner when we should pop leaf's. Tracing through the add_blocks calls should reveal whether the block stack is being populated correctly for nested blocks.\n\nWhen leaf.html renders, its ExtendsNode calls add_blocks with leaf's inner block, placing it at position 0 in blocks[inner]. Since leaf extends mid, we get mid.html's ExtendsNode, which then calls add_blocks with mid's outer and inner blocks. The issue likely emerges in how these blocks are stacked—specifically whether leaf's inner ends up in the right position relative to mid's inner when both target the same block name.\n\nAfter mid adds its blocks, we move to base.html (which has no ExtendsNode), and its blocks get added to the stack. The final blocks[inner] should be [base's inner, mid's inner, leaf's inner], so pop() retrieves leaf's inner correctly. But something in the add_blocks logic might be inserting blocks in an unexpected order or overwriting entries instead of appending them.\n\nLooking at the add_blocks method more carefully—it inserts each block at position 0, which means when multiple blocks are added sequentially, they stack in reverse order of insertion. If we're adding blocks from different templates in the inheritance chain, the order depends on when each template's blocks are processed and how they're being inserted into the existing lists. {"type":"assistant","message":{"model":"claude-haiku-4-5-20251001","id":"msg_01VKwHkFGhohTaZpcu5rEe44","type":"message","role":"assistant","content":[{"type":"thinking","thinking":"OK so push_state is a context manager that changes self.template and optionally pushes/pops the context. And importantly, in the ExtendsNode.render() code, it's called with `isolated_context=False`, which means it WON'T push/pop the context dict, just change the template reference.So the render_context should be shared across the push_state boundary!\n\nOK, so that's not the issue. Let me think about this differently...Actually, I think I need to consider what happens with the actual rendering of nested blocks more carefully. Let me trace through a concrete example with actual node objects.Say we have:\n- base.html: 1{% block outer %}BASE[{% block inner %}INNER_B{% endblock %}]{% endblock %}2\n- mid.html: {% extends \"base.html\" %}{% block outer %}MID[{% block inner %}INNER_M{% endblock %}]{% endblock %}\n- leaf.html: {% extends \"mid.html\" %}{% block inner %}INNER_L{% endblock %}When we parse these templates:base.html creates a template with nodelist:\n[TextNode(\"1\"), BlockNode(name=\"outer\", nodelist=[TextNode(\"BASE[\"), BlockNode(name=\"inner\", nodelist=[TextNode(\"INNER_B\")]), TextNode(\"]\")]), TextNode(\"2\")]\n\nmid.html creates a template with nodelist:\n[ExtendsNode(nodelist=[BlockNode(name=\"outer\", nodelist=[TextNode(\"MID[\"), BlockNode(name=\"inner\", nodelist=[TextNode(\"INNER_M\")]), TextNode(\"]\")]), ...]), ...]\n\nThe ExtendsNode of mid.html has blocks = {outer: BlockNode(outer) with nodelist containing inner, inner: BlockNode(inner) with nodelist [INNER_M]}\n\nleaf.html creates a template with nodelist:\n[ExtendsNode(nodelist=[BlockNode(name=\"inner\", nodelist=[TextNode(\"INNER_L\")]), ...]), ...]\n\n\nThe ExtendsNode of leaf.html has blocks = {inner: BlockNode(inner) with nodelist [INNER_L]}. When rendering leaf.html, Template._render() iterates through the nodelist and calls render() on each node. The first node is leaf.html's ExtendsNode, which registers its blocks (inner mapped to the BlockNode with nodelist [INNER_L]) into block_context.\n\nSince mid.html is the parent and also has an ExtendsNode, we skip adding its blocks directly. Instead, mid.html gets rendered with push_state(). Its ExtendsNode then registers both outer and inner blocks, with inner becoming a list containing the BlockNode from mid.html followed by the one from leaf.html (inserted at position 0). When we reach base.html as the parent, it has no ExtendsNode, so we proceed to add its blocks to the context.\n\nBase.html's blocks get registered next: outer now contains both the base and mid versions, while inner accumulates all three versions in order. We render base.html with push_state(), and as its nodes are processed, the TextNode outputs \"1\". When we encounter the outer BlockNode, block_context.pop(\"outer\") retrieves the mid.html version, creating a new BlockNode that will render with mid's content instead of base's.\n\nThe inner BlockNode from mid.html then gets rendered—block_context.pop(\"inner\") returns the last registered version (INNER_L), so that becomes the active content, outputting \"INNER_L\". This cascades back up through mid.html's outer block, producing \"MID[INNER_L]\", and the process continues as we push the base version back onto the stack.\n\nBack in base.html's template, after processing the outer block override, we encounter the final TextNode(\"2\"), which renders as \"2\". So the complete output becomes: \"1\" + \"MID[INNER_L]\" + \"2\".\n\nThis confirms the inner block is correctly overridden by the leaf template's version. But there's a critical detail about how pop() works—it removes from the end of the blocks list, so when we have multiple registrations of the same block, we're getting the most recently added one.\n\nIf the order of block registrations were different, say [INNER_B, INNER_L, INNER_M], then pop() would grab INNER_M instead of INNER_L, causing the wrong content to render. This suggests the bug might stem from how blocks are being added during the parsing of mid.html. The order in which add_blocks gets called when extracting blocks from the extending template matters significantly.\n\nWhen mid.html is parsed, its BlockNode(inner) containing INNER_M gets registered first. Then base.html's blocks are added, placing BlockNode(inner) with INNER_B at the front of the list. The issue is that when we later pop from blocks[inner], we're getting whichever block was added last, not necessarily the one we intended.\n\nThe core problem: if blocks[inner] ends up ordered as [INNER_B, INNER_M, INNER_L], popping gives us INNER_L correctly. But the actual output shows INNER_M leaking through, suggesting the block ordering or selection logic isn't working as expected. The middle template's content shouldn't be visible when the innermost template provides an override.\n\nLooking at add_blocks, it inserts blocks at position 0, which means the order depends on dictionary iteration order. Since Python 3.7+ maintains insertion order, the sequence matters—it's determined by how get_nodes_by_type traverses the nodelist and builds the blocks dictionary. The order of BlockNodes in the original template structure directly affects which block gets rendered when we pop from the stack. it first checks if the current node matches the type, appending it if so, then recursively searches through all child_nodelists. This means outer BlockNodes get added before inner ones since the recursion happens after checking the current node. So for a structure with an outer BlockNode containing an inner BlockNode, the method returns them in the correct order: outer first, then inner.\n\nWhen mid.html's ExtendsNode creates its blocks dictionary, they're processed in insertion order—outer gets added to the blocks list first via insert(0, outer), then inner via insert(0, inner). Later when base.html's blocks are added, the same insertion pattern applies, placing base's outer block before mid's outer block in the final list.\n\nLet me search the codebase for any existing issues or comments that might point to what's going wrong.","signature":""}],"stop_reason":null,"stop_sequence":null,"usage":{"input_tokens":7,"cache_creation_input_tokens":497,"cache_read_input_tokens":59604,"cache_creation":{"ephemeral_5m_input_tokens":0,"ephemeral_1h_input_tokens":497},"output_tokens":1,"service_tier":"standard","inference_geo":"not_available"},"context_management":null},"parent_tool_use_id":null,"session_id":"9289f73d-547f-4b33-bcf6-616cce479223","uuid":"270d5a64-ba35-488a-ab5a-49465b32a440"}

The current template's blocks are added first, then the parent's blocks are extracted from the parent template and added. This ordering matters - if blocks from intermediate templates are being added at the wrong time relative to leaf or root blocks, content could leak through or get overridden incorrectly.\n\nWhen rendering a chain like leaf.html → mid.html → base.html, the flow is: leaf adds its blocks and renders mid without adding mid's blocks yet; then mid adds its own blocks and, since base has no extends node, also adds base's blocks before rendering base. This ensures each level's blocks are available when needed without duplication.\n\nThe critical insight is understanding how block insertion works—when mid's blocks are added at position 0, they stack before leaf's blocks in the same named block. But there's a subtle issue: mid's inner block node exists within mid's outer block node. When rendering mid's outer block, the nodelist gets popped and executed, which means mid's inner block node is encountered and processed during that render cycle. This creates a timing problem where the inner block might be rendered before all the parent blocks have been properly registered.\n\nThe real problem appears to be that blocks[inner] shouldn't contain mid's own inner block at the point when mid's inner is being rendered—it should only hold overrides from templates higher in the hierarchy. If blocks[inner] incorrectly includes mid's inner alongside base's and leaf's, the pop() operation returns the wrong block, causing mid's content to leak through instead of being properly overridden.\n\nReconsidering the structure: when rendering mid's nodelist, blocks[inner] should only contain leaf's inner, not the full chain. This way, the first pop() call returns leaf's inner correctly, and subsequent calls would find nothing, allowing the default block to render as needed.\n\nThe core issue might stem from collecting blocks across too many template levels. Re-examining the problem statement suggests the innermost template overrides a nested block structure—meaning it overrides both an outer block and an inner block contained within it, like in a base.html template where one block nests inside another.\n\nTracing through a concrete example: if leaf.html extends mid.html which extends base.html, and each level overrides both outer and inner blocks, then the blocks dictionary accumulates entries from all three templates. When rendering leaf's outer block's nodelist, popping inner should return leaf's version first, then mid's version, then base's version. But the rendering context matters—when we're inside leaf's outer block, we should only see leaf's inner override, not the full chain from all templates.\n\nThe core issue likely stems from how blocks are being stored or retrieved during the inheritance chain. It could be that the dictionary comprehension is colliding with identically-named blocks, the iteration order is incorrect, or blocks from different template levels are interfering with each other. The parent parameter in BlockNode might also be playing a role in how the block resolution works. The parent field isn't actually being used during rendering, which suggests the block inheritance tracking might be incomplete. When blocks are nested inside other blocks, they should only be relevant when their parent block gets rendered, so extracting them the same way as top-level blocks could be the problem. I should try extracting only the direct children of the nodelist rather than pulling out all nested blocks indiscriminately.","signature":""}],"stop_reason":null,"stop_sequence":null,"usage":{"input_tokens":7,"cache_creation_input_tokens":282,"cache_read_input_tokens":70412,"cache_creation":{"ephemeral_5m_input_tokens":0,"ephemeral_1h_input_tokens":282},"output_tokens":1,"service_tier":"standard","inference_geo":"not_available"},"context_management":null},"parent_tool_use_id":null,"session_id":"9289f73d-547f-4b33-bcf6-616cce479223","uuid":"53f3ccbe-4d8d-42a9-be2b-588cd8c89553"}

{"type":"assistant","message":{"model":"claude-haiku-4-5-20251001","id":"msg_01PUeMFuXncMPdtwfi5oa5Z7","type":"message","role":"assistant","content":[{"type":"thinking","thinking":"The problem is on line 94: `self.blocks = {n.name: n for n in nodelist.get_nodes_by_type(BlockNode)}`\n\nThis extracts ALL BlockNodes from the nodelist, including nested ones. But this is the problem! When a template extends another and defines blocks, only the TOP-LEVEL blocks should be considered as overrides. Nested blocks that are part of the parent template's structure should not be extracted here.\n\nHowever, I need to be careful. What if a child template overrides a block that was nested in the parent? In that case, the child can override it at the top level of the child template.\n\nLet me think about this more carefully:\n- base.html: {% block outer %}BASE[{% block inner %}INNER_B{% endblock %}]{% endblock %}\n- mid.html: {% extends \"base.html\" %}{% block inner %}INNER_M{% endblock %}\n\nIn mid.html, inner is defined at the top level, so it should override base.html's inner block.\n\nWhen we parse mid.html, we get:\n- nodelist = [BlockNode(inner) [INNER_M]]\n\nAnd get_nodes_by_type(BlockNode) returns [BlockNode(inner)]\n\nSo mid.blocks = {inner: BlockNode(inner) [INNER_M]}\n\nNow, when we add this to block_context:\n- blocks[inner] = [BlockNode(inner) [INNER_M]]\n\nAnd when we render base.html's inner (which is inside base.html's outer), we pop from blocks[inner] and should get mid's inner.\n\nThat's correct!\n\nSo the logic of extracting ALL BlockNodes seems correct.Unless... unless the problem is that we're extracting mid's NESTED blocks in addition to mid's TOP-LEVEL blocks!\n\nFor example:\n- mid.html: {% extends \"base.html\" %}{% block outer %}MID[{% block inner %}INNER_M{% endblock %}]{% endblock %}\n\n\n- nodelist = [BlockNode(outer) containing BlockNode(inner)]\n- get_nodes_by_type(BlockNode) returns [BlockNode(outer), BlockNode(inner)]\n- mid.blocks = {outer: BlockNode(outer), inner: BlockNode(inner) (inside outer)}\n\nWhen these get added to block_context, both outer and inner are registered. The issue is that inner is nested inside outer, so when outer gets rendered, it will use the inner BlockNode from mid that's already inside it. But we're also registering inner separately in the block_context, which could cause conflicts when rendering base.html's structure.\n\nWhen rendering base.html's outer block, we pop mid's outer and render its nodelist, which contains mid's inner. When we then render mid's inner, we pop from block_context and get mid's inner. But this happens before leaf.html's override is processed, so the block_context[inner] list would be [BlockNode(inner) from leaf, BlockNode(inner) from mid]. The insertion order matters here—leaf's override gets priority. Let me trace through the full three-level inheritance to see exactly how this resolves.\n\nStarting with the template structure: base.html defines outer and inner blocks, mid.html extends base and overrides both blocks (with inner nested inside outer), and leaf.html extends mid but only overrides inner at the top level. During parsing, each template builds its own blocks dictionary—mid captures its inner block nested within outer, while leaf captures its top-level inner block. When leaf renders, it first processes its ExtendsNode, which adds leaf's inner block to the block_context before moving to the parent template.\n\nNext, mid's ExtendsNode processes, adding mid's outer and inner blocks to the context. Since leaf already provided an inner block, mid's inner gets inserted before it in the stack. Then base's blocks are added, creating the final hierarchy: blocks[outer] contains base's then mid's outer, and blocks[inner] contains base's, mid's, then leaf's inner blocks in order. During base.html's render, BlockNode(outer) pops mid's outer from the stack and renders its content—the text \"MID[\" followed by the nested BlockNode(inner), which then pops and renders leaf's inner block, producing the final output.\n\nThe key insight is that the dictionary iteration order matters. When mid.blocks is created from the nodelist containing BlockNode(outer) and BlockNode(inner), get_nodes_by_type returns them in that sequence, so the dict maintains outer before inner. This ordering determines how blocks are added to the context during the add_blocks call.\n\nBut there's a potential issue if get_nodes_by_type actually returns nodes in a different order than expected. Looking at the implementation, it iterates through the nodelist and collects nodes recursively, which could reverse the nesting order depending on how the tree is structured.\n\nLet me trace through what happens: for a nodelist containing TextNode, BlockNode(outer), and another TextNode, the method would skip the first TextNode, then match and collect BlockNode(outer) along with any nested BlockNode(inner) from recursion, giving [BlockNode(outer), BlockNode(inner)]. That matches what I'd expect.\n\nSo the traversal logic seems sound. Maybe the real problem lies elsewhere—perhaps in how the blocks dictionary is constructed or populated. If blocks with identical names appear multiple times, the dictionary comprehension could be silently overwriting earlier entries.\n\nActually, I wonder if the core issue is something different. When rendering mid.html, we add mid's blocks first, then base's blocks. But base's inner BlockNode exists inside base's outer block. When we render base's outer, we pop mid's outer (which contains mid's inner), and then rendering that inner should pop from blocks[inner]—which should have leaf's inner queued up. Unless there's a bug in how blocks are being added or removed from the stack, or in the BlockNode.render() method itself where new block instances are created.\n\nLooking at the render logic more carefully: when we pop a block and it's None, we fall back to self, then create a new instance with `type(self)(block.name, block.nodelist)`. If block was None, we're using self's name and nodelist to construct this new instance. The new block gets the current context, its nodelist renders, and if we had popped something earlier, we push it back. This flow seems right, but maybe the issue is that we're not properly handling the case where block is None—we might be losing track of which nodelist should actually render. The `parent` parameter defaults to `None` in BlockNode's `__init__`, so omitting it shouldn't cause problems. Looking at the broader picture, the `parent` field doesn't seem to be actively used anywhere in the codebase, so that's likely not the root cause.\n\nThe real issue appears to be that nested blocks are being extracted and added to the block_context indiscriminately. I should focus on extracting only top-level blocks from the nodelist, filtering out any that are nested within other blocks. {"type":"assistant","message":{"model":"claude-haiku-4-5-20251001","id":"msg_01UrytATBLCUUDPFxSZNh3nn","type":"message","role":"assistant","content":[{"type":"thinking","thinking":"Now let me think about what the fix should be. The issue is that when we have 3 levels of inheritance with nested blocks, the block resolution gets confused.\n\nLooking at the code more carefully, I think the issue might be related to how the blocks are added to the BlockContext when there are nested blocks involved.\n\nThe key insight is this: when a template has nested blocks (a block inside another block), and the child template overrides only the nested block, we need to make sure we're using the right override when the nested block is rendered.\n\nThe current code adds ALL blocks from a template to the block_context, including nested ones. But this might cause issues because:\n1. The nested blocks are added at the same level as top-level blocks\n2. When the parent block is rendered, it contains a reference to the original nested block, not the override\n\nLet me think about how to fix this...Actually, I think the issue might be more subtle. Let me consider what should happen:\n\n1. When a template extends another and defines blocks, those block definitions should override the parent's blocks\n2. But nested blocks (blocks inside other blocks) should not be extracted as top-level overrides\n\nSo the fix might be to only extract TOP-LEVEL blocks from a template's nodelist, not nested ones.\n\nBut wait, that wouldn't work for the case where a child template defines a nested block at the top level to override a nested block in the parent.\n\nActually, I think I need to understand the intended behavior better. Let me think about what SHOULD happen:\n\nWhen leaf.html extends mid.html and defines:\n```{% block inner %}INNER_L{% endblock %}\n```\n\nThis should override the inner block wherever it appears in mid.html's template.\n\nBut the issue is that there are MULTIPLE inner blocks:\n1. The inner block inside mid.html's outer block (from mid.html)\n2. The inner block inside base.html's outer block (from base.html)\n\nWhen we render the nested inner block that's inside mid.html's outer block, we should get leaf's inner content, not mid's inner content.\n\n\nThe problem is in how blocks get stacked in the context. When extracting blocks from mid.html, we capture mid's inner block (which is nested inside outer), but then when leaf's inner block is added, the ordering becomes ambiguous. The real issue is that we're not properly tracking which inner block belongs to which outer block, so when rendering, we can't distinguish between the nested inner blocks from different template levels. outer first at position 0, then inner at position 0, so inner ends up before outer in the blocks list. When iterating through the dict items, the order depends on how get_nodes_by_type returns the nodes—if it returns them in a specific sequence, that determines the insertion order into our blocks list. The key issue is ensuring we're popping from the correct end of the queue when multiple inner blocks exist at different nesting levels. not the order of get_nodes_by_type.\n\nLet me try a completely different angle. Maybe the issue is in how the blocks are being POPPED, not how they're being ADDED. What if we're adding blocks from the parent BEFORE we add blocks from the child in some cases? In ExtendsNode.render(), we always add self.blocks first (line 142), and then we add parent's blocks (line 154). So the order should be correct. Unless we're calling ExtendsNode.render() in the wrong order for some reason? Let me re-read the render sequence more carefully. When we call leaf.html.render(): 1. leaf.html's Template._render() is called 2. It iterates through leaf.html's nodelist: [ExtendsNode,\n3. ExtendsNode.render() is called, which adds leaf.blocks, gets mid.html as parent, sees mid has ExtendsNode so doesn't add mid's blocks yet, then pushes state and calls mid.html._render()\n4. mid.html._render() is called with the same context, iterates through its nodelist, and mid's ExtendsNode.render() adds mid.blocks, gets base.html as parent, sees base has no ExtendsNode so adds base's blocks, then pushes state and calls base.html._render()\n5. base.html._render() is called with the same context, confirming the add_blocks order is leaf, then mid, then base.\n\nThe blocks are being extracted correctly in sequence, but I suspect the real issue is how nested blocks within a single template are handled. When a template contains blocks nested inside other blocks—like an inner block wrapped in an outer block—both get extracted as top-level overrides instead of preserving their nesting relationship. Dictionary of FIFO queues.\n        self.blocks = defaultdict(list)\n\n    def __repr__(self):\n        return f\"<{self.__class__.__qualname__}: blocks={self.blocks!r}>\"\n\n    def add_blocks(self, blocks):\n        for name, block in blocks.items():\n            self.blocks[name].insert(0, block)\n\n    def pop(self, name):\n        try:\n            return self.blocks[name].pop()\n        except IndexError:\n            return None\n\n    def push(self, name, block):\n        self.blocks[name].append(block)\n\n    def get_block(self, name):\n        try:\n            return self.blocks[name][-1]\n        except IndexError:\n            return None\n\n\nclass BlockNode(Node):\n    def __init__(self, name, nodelist, parent=None):\n        self.name = name\n        self.nodelist = nodelist\n        self.parent = parent\n\n    def __repr__(self):\n        return \"<Block Node: %s. A history is kept in\n        the render_context attribute between successive extends calls and\n        passed as the skip argument. This enables extends to work recursively\n        without extending the same template twice.\n        \"\"\"\n        history = context.render_context.setdefault(\n            self.context_key,\n            [self.origin],\n        )\n        template, origin = context.template.engine.find_template(\n            template_name,\n            skip=history,\n        )\n        history.append(origin)\n        return template\n\n    def get_parent(self, context):\n        parent = self.parent_name.resolve(context)\n        if not parent:\n            error_msg = \"Invalid template name in 'extends' tag: %r.\" % parent\n            if self.parent_name.filters or isinstance(self.parent_name.var, Variable):\n                error_msg += (\n                    \" Got this from the '%s' variable.\" % self.parent_name.token\n                )\n            raise TemplateSyntaxError(error_msg)\n        if isinstance(parent, Template):\n            # parent is a django.template.Template\n            return parent\n        if isinstance(getattr(parent, \"template\", None), Template):\n            # parent is a django.template.backends.django.Template\n            return parent.template\n        return self.find_template(parent, context)\n\n    def render(self, context):\n        compiled_parent = self.get_parent(context)\n\n        if BLOCK_CONTEXT_KEY not in context.render_context:\n            context.render_context[BLOCK_CONTEXT_KEY] = BlockContext()\n        block_context = context.render_context[BLOCK_CONTEXT_KEY]\n\n        # Add the block nodes from this node to the block context\n        block_context.add_blocks(self.blocks)\n\n        # If this block's parent doesn't have an extends node it is the root,\n        # and its block nodes also need to be added to the block context.\n        for node in compiled_parent.nodelist:\n            # The ExtendsNode has to be the first non-text node.\n            if not isinstance(node, TextNode):\n                if not isinstance(node, ExtendsNode):\n                    blocks = {\n                        n.name: n\n                        for n in compiled_parent.nodelist.get_nodes_by_type(BlockNode)\n                    }\n                    block_context.add_blocks(blocks)\n                break\n\n        # Call Template._render explicitly so the parser context stays\n        # the same.\n        with context.render_context.push_state(compiled_parent, isolated_context=False):\n            return compiled_parent._render(context)\n\n\nclass IncludeNode(Node):\n    context_key = \"__include_context\"\n\n    def __init__(\n        self, template, *args, extra_context=None, isolated_context=False, **kwargs\n    ):\n        self.template = template\n        self.extra_context = extra_context or {}\n        self.isolated_context = isolated_context\n        super().__init__(*args, **kwargs)\n\n    def __repr__(self):\n        return f\"<{self.__class__.__qualname__}: template={self.template!r}>\"\n\n    def render(self, context):\n        \"\"\"\n        Render the specified template and context. Cache the template object\n        in render_context to avoid reparsing and loading when used in a for\n        loop.\n        \"\"\"\n        template = self.template.resolve(context)\n        # Does this quack like a Template?\n        if not callable(getattr(template, \"render\", None)):\n            # Use the base.Template of a backends.django.Template.\n        elif hasattr(template, \"template\"):\n            template = template.template\n        values = {\n            name: var.resolve(context) for name, var in self.extra_context.items()\n        }\n        if self.isolated_context:\n            return template.render(context.new(values))\n        with context.push(**values):\n            return template.render(context)\n\n\n@register.tag(\"block\")\ndef do_block(parser, token):\n    \"\"\"\n    Define a block that can be overridden by child templates.\n    \"\"\"\n    # token.split_contents() isn't useful here because this tag doesn't accept\n    # variable as arguments.\n    bits = token.contents.split()\n    if len(bits) != 2:\n        raise TemplateSyntaxError(\"'%s' tag takes only one argument\" % bits[0])\n    block_name = bits[1]\n    # Keep track of the names of BlockNodes found in this template, so we can\n    # check for duplication.\n    try:\n        if block_name in parser.__loaded_blocks:\n            raise TemplateSyntaxError(\n                \"'%s' tag with name '%s' appears more than once\" % (bits[0], block_name)\n            )\n        parser.__loaded_blocks.append(block_name)\n    except AttributeError:  # parser.__loaded_blocks isn't a list yet\n        parser.__loaded_blocks = [block_name]\n    nodelist = parser.parse((\"endblock\",))\n\n    # This check is kept for backwards-compatibility. See #3100.\n    endblock = parser.next_token()\n    acceptable_endblocks = (\"endblock\", \"endblock %s\" % block_name)\n    if endblock.contents not in acceptable_endblocks:\n        parser.invalid_block_tag(endblock, \"endblock\", acceptable_endblocks)\n\n    return BlockNode(block_name, nodelist)\n\n\ndef construct_relative_path(\n    current_template_name,\n    relative_name,\n    allow_recursion=False,\n):\n    \"\"\"\n    Convert a relative path (starting with './' or '../') to the full template\n    name based on the current_template_name.\n    \"\"\"\n    new_name = relative_name.strip(\"'\\\"\")\n    if not new_name.startswith((\"./\", \"../\")):\n        # relative_name is a variable or a literal that doesn't contain a\n        # relative path.\n        return relative_name\n\n    if current_template_name is None:\n        # Unknown origin (e.g. Template('...').render(Context({...})).\n        raise TemplateSyntaxError(\n            f\"The relative path {relative_name} cannot be evaluated due to \"\n            \"an unknown template origin.\"\n        )\n\n    new_name = posixpath.normpath(\n        posixpath.join(\n            posixpath.dirname(current_template_name.lstrip(\"/\")),\n            new_name,\n        )\n    )\n    if new_name.startswith(\"../\"):\n        raise TemplateSyntaxError(\n            \"The relative path '%s' points outside the file hierarchy that \"\n            \"template '%s' is in.\" % (relative_name, current_template_name)\n        )\n    if not allow_recursion and current_template_name.lstrip(\"/\") == new_name:\n        raise TemplateSyntaxError(\n            \"The relative path '%s' was translated to template name '%s', the \"\n            \"same template in which the tag appears.\"\n            % (relative_name, current_template_name)\n        )\n    has_quotes = (\n        relative_name.startswith(('\"', \"'\")) and relative_name[0] == relative_name[-1]\n    )\n    return f'\"{new_name}\"' if has_quotes else new_name\n\n\n@register.tag(\"extends\")\ndef do_extends(parser, token):\n    \"\"\"\n    Signal that this template extends a parent template.\n\n    This tag may be used in two ways: ``{% extends \"base\" %}`` (with quotes)\n    uses the literal value \"base\" as the name of the parent template to extend,\n    or ``{% But the issue is that add_blocks() uses insert(0, block) to add blocks, which inserts at the beginning of the list.\n\n\nSo when we have nested extends, the order gets reversed. If we start with blocks[inner] = [base's inner, mid's inner], then calling add_blocks({inner: leaf's inner}) inserts at position 0, giving us [leaf's inner, base's inner, mid's inner]. Since pop() removes from the end, we'd be popping mid's inner first instead of leaf's inner, which would cause mid's content to leak through.\n\nThe real issue is that insert(0, ...) puts new items at the front while pop() takes from the back—so we're getting items in reverse order. The fix requires reversing how we store blocks so they go from root to leaf, ensuring the correct popping sequence. () should return leaf's inner, which is correct!\n\nLet me trace through the actual sequence of add_blocks calls. In ExtendsNode.render(), we add self.blocks first, then conditionally add parent's blocks, then render the parent. The mid.blocks dictionary has both the outer and inner blocks, but the inner block is already part of the outer block's nodelist. This could cause the wrong block to be used when rendering. When `parser.parse()` is called, it parses everything after the extends tag, so for mid.html it captures the block definitions that follow. The `mid.ExtendsNode.nodelist` contains those parsed blocks, which means the block hierarchy is properly constructed during parsing.\n\nThe critical insight is what happens during rendering. When mid.html is rendered, the ExtendsNode doesn't render mid.html's own blocks directly. Instead, it registers those blocks in the block_context and then renders the parent template, allowing the parent's blocks to be overridden. So the execution flow is: mid.html's render method encounters the ExtendsNode, which adds mid's blocks to the context and then renders base.html using those overrides.\n\nWhen base.html renders its BlockNode(outer), it retrieves the override from block_context—which is mid's version of that block.
