{"type":"assistant","message":{"model":"claude-haiku-4-5-20251001","id":"msg_01JMidUKE3bXbUMHsNAx7DRd","type":"message","role":"assistant","content":[{"type":"thinking","thinking":"Now I see the issue! The comment says \"Dictionary of FIFO queues\" but the implementation is actually a LIFO queue with mixed operations:\n\n- add_blocks uses insert(0, ...) which adds to the BEGINNING\n- pop() uses pop() which removes from the END\n- push() uses append() which adds to the END\n- get_block() uses [-1] which gets the LAST item\n\nSo we have:\n- insert(0, ...) at the beginning\n- append() at the end\n- pop() from the end\n- get() the last itemThis is actually a LIFO stack, not a FIFO queue!The confusion is:\n1. insert(0, block) adds to the front\n2. append(block) adds to the back\n3. pop() removes from the back\n\nSo if we do:\n1. insert(0, A): [A]\n2. insert(0, B): [B, A]\n3. insert(0, C): [C, B, A]\n4. pop(): returns A (from the back)\n\nBut wait, if we pop from [C, B, A], we get A, which is correct! We want to use the most recently overridden block, which is A (the leaf block).\n\nSo the logic is still sound...UNLESS we're calling operations in the wrong order!\n\nActually, wait. Let me reconsider. If insert(0, block) inserts at the BEGINNING, and pop() removes from the END:\n\n1. insert(0, A): [A]\n2. insert(0, B): [B, A]\n3. insert(0, C): [C, B, A]\n4. pop(): removes the LAST item (index -1), which is A, returns A\n\nSo pop() returns A, which was the FIRST block we inserted!\n\nThat means pop() returns the OLDEST override, not the NEWEST!\n\nThat's the bug!\n\nThe problem is that insert(0, ...) and pop() don't work together correctly as a stack. If you insert at the front and pop from the back, you get LIFO behavior, but the items are in the wrong order!\n\n\nThe fix requires choosing between two approaches: either switch insert(0, ...) to append(...) so new blocks go to the end, or change pop() to pop(0) to remove from the front. For proper LIFO semantics where the newest override is accessible, append() with pop() creates the right structure. Contents: %r>\" % (self.name, self.nodelist)\n\n    def render(self, context):\n        block_context = context.render_context.get(BLOCK_CONTEXT_KEY)\n        with context.push():\n            if block_context is None:\n                context[\"block\"] = self\n                result = self.nodelist.render(context)\n            else:\n                push = block = block_context.pop(self.name)\n                if block is None:\n                    block = self\n                # Create new block so we can store context without\n                # thread-safety issues.\n                block = type(self)(block.name, block.nodelist)\n                block.context = context\n                context[\"block\"] = block\n                result = block.nodelist.render(context)\n                if push is not None:\n                    block_context.push(self.name, push)\n        return result\n\n    def super(self):\n        if not hasattr(self, \"context\"):\n            raise TemplateSyntaxError(\n                \"'%s' object has no attribute 'context'. A history is kept in\n        the render_context attribute between successive extends calls and\n        passed as the skip argument. This enables extends to work recursively\n        without extending the same template twice.\n        \"\"\"\n        history = context.render_context.setdefault(\n            self.context_key,\n            [self.origin],\n        )\n        template, origin = context.template.engine.find_template(\n            template_name,\n            skip=history,\n        )\n        history.append(origin)\n        return template\n\n    def get_parent(self, context):\n        parent = self.parent_name.resolve(context)\n        if not parent:\n            error_msg = \"Invalid template name in 'extends' tag: %r.\" % parent\n            if self.parent_name.filters or isinstance(self.parent_name.var, Variable):\n                error_msg += (\n                    \" Got this from the '%s' variable.\" % self.parent_name.token\n                )\n            raise TemplateSyntaxError(error_msg)\n        if isinstance(parent, Template):\n            # parent is a django.template.Template\n            return parent\n        if isinstance(getattr(parent, \"template\", None), Template):\n            # parent is a django.template.backends.django.Template\n            return parent.template\n        return self.find_template(parent, context)\n\n    def render(self, context):\n        compiled_parent = self.get_parent(context)\n\n        if BLOCK_CONTEXT_KEY not in context.render_context:\n            context.render_context[BLOCK_CONTEXT_KEY] = BlockContext()\n        block_context = context.render_context[BLOCK_CONTEXT_KEY]\n\n        # Add the block nodes from this node to the block context\n        block_context.add_blocks(self.blocks)\n\n        # If this block's parent doesn't have an extends node it is the root,\n        # and its block nodes also need to be added to the block context.\n        for node in compiled_parent.nodelist:\n            # Now blocks are appended to the end of the list, and pop() removes from the end, which gives us proper LIFO (Last In, First Out) semantics.\n\nLet me trace through the logic again with this change:\n\nWhen rendering leaf.html -> mid.html -> base.html:1. leaf.ExtendsNode.render() adds leaf.blocks:\n   - blocks[inner].append(leaf's inner): blocks[inner] = [leaf's inner]2. mid.ExtendsNode.render() adds mid.blocks:\n   - blocks[inner].append(mid's inner): blocks[inner] = [leaf's inner, mid's inner]   - blocks[outer].append(mid's outer): blocks[outer] = [mid's outer]3. mid.ExtendsNode.render() adds base.blocks:\n   - blocks[inner].append(base's inner): blocks[inner] = [leaf's inner, mid's inner, base's inner]   - blocks[outer].append(base's outer): blocks[outer] = [mid's outer, base's outer]When rendering base.html:\n1. With LIFO semantics, this means we should add them in REVERSE order: [base, mid, leaf].\n\n\nThe issue is that blocks get added in the inheritance chain order (leaf → mid → base), but with a stack we need the most specific override (leaf) on top. So I should add them in reverse: base first, then mid, then leaf.    allow_recursion=False,\n):\n    \"\"\"\n    Convert a relative path (starting with './' or '../') to the full template\n    name based on the current_template_name.\n    \"\"\"\n    new_name = relative_name.strip(\"'\\\"\")\n    if not new_name.startswith((\"./\", \"../\")):\n        # relative_name is a variable or a literal that doesn't contain a\n        # relative path.\n        return relative_name\n\n    if current_template_name is None:\n        # Unknown origin (e.g. Template('...').render(Context({...})).\n        raise TemplateSyntaxError(\n            f\"The relative path path '%s' points outside the file hierarchy that \"\n            \"template '%s' is in.\" % (relative_name, current_template_name)\n        )\n    if not allow_recursion and current_template_name.lstrip(\"/\") == new_name:\n        raise TemplateSyntaxError(\n            \"The relative path '%s' was translated to template name '%s', the \"\n            \"same template in which the tag appears.\"\n            % (relative_name, current_template_name)\n        )\n    has_quotes = (\n        relative_name.startswith(('\"', \"'\")) and relative_name[0] == relative_name[-1]\n    )\n    return f'\"{new_name}\"' if has_quotes else new_name\n\n\n@register.tag(\"extends\")\ndef do_extends(parser, token):\n    \"\"\"\n    Signal that this template extends a parent template.\n\n    This tag may be used in two ways: ``{% extends \"base\" %}`` (with quotes)\n    uses the literal value \"base\" as the name of the parent template to extend,\n    or ``{% extends variable %}`` uses the value of ``variable`` as either the\n    name of the parent template to extend (if it evaluates to a string) or as\n    the parent template itself (if it evaluates to a The issue might be with how blocks are added to the block_context in ExtendsNode.render(). Specifically, when we add the parent's blocks:\n\n```python\nblocks = {\n    n.name: n\n    for n in compiled_parent.nodelist.get_nodes_by_type(BlockNode)\n}\nblock_context.add_blocks(blocks)\n```\n\nThis gets ALL BlockNodes from the parent, including nested ones. But when we render, we're rendering the nodelist of popped blocks, which may contain NESTED blocks that have already been extracted and added to the block_context.\n\nSo when we have:\n- base.html's outer contains base.html's inner- mid.html's outer contains mid.html's inner- leaf.html defines inner at the top level\n\nWhen we render mid.html's outer (which we popped from the block_context), its nodelist contains mid.html's inner BlockNode. When we render this inner BlockNode, it calls render(), which pops from the block_context.\n\nAt this point, blocks[inner] contains [base's inner, mid's inner, leaf's inner], and pop() should return leaf's inner. blocks are stored with parent first, child last\n        self.blocks = defaultdict(list)\n\n    def __repr__(self):\n        return f\"<{self.__class__.__qualname__}: blocks={self.blocks!r}>\"\n\n    def add_blocks(self, blocks):\n        for name, block in blocks.items():\n            self.blocks[name].insert(0, block)\n\n    def pop(self, name):\n        try:\n            return self.blocks[name].pop()\n        except IndexError:\n            return None\n\n    def push(self, name, block):\n        self.blocks[name].append(block)\n\n    def get_block(self, name):\n        try:\n            return self.blocks[name][-1]\n        except IndexError:\n            return None\n\n\nclass BlockNode(Node):\n    def __init__(self, name, nodelist, parent=None):\n        self.name = name\n        self.nodelist = nodelist\n        self.parent = parent\n\n    def __repr__(self):\n        return \"<Block Node: %s. parent template.\n\n    This tag may be used in two ways: ``{% extends \"base\" %}`` (with quotes)\n    uses the literal value \"base\" as the name of the parent template to extend,\n    or ``{% extends variable %}`` uses the value of ``variable`` as either the\n    name of the parent template to extend (if it evaluates to a string) or as\n    the parent template itself (if it evaluates to a It's a direct child of the nodelist (top-level), OR\n        # 2. A history is kept in\n        the render_context attribute between successive extends calls and\n        passed as the skip argument. This enables extends to work recursively\n        without extending the same template twice.\n        \"\"\"\n        history = context.render_context.setdefault(\n            self.context_key,\n            [self.origin],\n        )\n        template, origin = context.template.engine.find_template(\n            template_name,\n            skip=history,\n        )\n        history.append(origin)\n        return template\n\n    def get_parent(self, context):\n        parent = self.parent_name.resolve(context)\n        if not parent:\n            error_msg = \"Invalid template name in 'extends' tag: %r.\" % parent\n            if self.parent_name.filters or isinstance(self.parent_name.var, Variable):\n                error_msg += (\n                    \" Got this from the '%s' variable.\" % self.parent_name.token\n                )\n            raise TemplateSyntaxError(error_msg)\n        if isinstance(parent, Template):\n            # Let me check...Actually, we handle that on line 60:\n```python\nif block is None:\n    block = self\n```\n\nSo if the pop returns None, we use self instead. Then we create a new block from either the popped block or self.\n\nBut there's something subtle here. We pop a block, which gives us an OVERRIDE block from a child template. We create a new instance of that block. Then we render it.\n\nThe issue is that we're rendering the OVERRIDE block's nodelist, which contains the override content. That's correct.\n\nBut when the override block contains nested blocks, those nested blocks are part of the override block's nodelist. So when we render them, they'll also call pop() and potentially get the wrong override.\n\n\nWhen mid.html defines a block with nested blocks inside it, those nested BlockNode instances are part of mid.html's nodelist. When we pop and render mid.html's outer block, we're rendering its nodelist which includes the nested blocks from mid.html, not from base.html. These nested blocks are different instances than the ones in base.html's outer block, but they're the same instances that were parsed from mid.html during parsing.\n\nWhen mid's inner block renders, it should retrieve the most specific override from block_context—which would be leaf's inner block since leaf is the most derived template. The logic seems sound unless there's an issue with how blocks are stored or retrieved from block_context. I'm wondering if the dictionary iteration order or the way blocks are being indexed in the dict comprehension could be causing problems. When we build the blocks dictionary using `{n.name: n for n in nodelist.get_nodes_by_type(BlockNode)}`, the order depends on what get_nodes_by_type returns, and if that order differs from expectations, it could affect which block override gets selected.\n\nLet me trace through the actual insertion sequence. If get_nodes_by_type returns blocks in order [outer, inner], then mid's blocks dict will have items in that same order. When add_blocks iterates through mid's blocks and inserts each one at position 0, mid's inner ends up at the front of the blocks[inner] list. Then when base's blocks are added the same way, base's inner gets inserted at position 0, pushing mid's inner back. The final stack becomes [base's inner, mid's inner, leaf's inner], and pop() correctly returns leaf's inner—the most specific override. The mechanism appears to be working as intended.\n\nBut there might be something else going on with how nested blocks at different hierarchy levels interact during rendering. {"type":"assistant","message":{"model":"claude-haiku-4-5-20251001","id":"msg_01XAE2xVC3aZsLTrpesHuHbB","type":"message","role":"assistant","content":[{"type":"thinking","thinking":"Now let me think about what the actual bug might be based on the problem description. The issue is that in 3+ levels with nested blocks, the middle template's content leaks through.\n\nLooking at the code flow again, when we have:- level2 defines {% block inner %}out{% endblock %} at top-level\n- level1 defines {% block first %}2{% block inner %}inner{% endblock %}{% endblock %} at top-level\n- base01 defines {% block first %}&{% endblock %}\n\nWhen level2 is rendered:\n1. level2.ExtendsNode.render() adds level2.blocks = {inner: level2's inner}2. Renders level1\n3. level1.ExtendsNode.render() adds level1.blocks = {first: level1's first, inner: level1's inner}4. level1 does NOT extend base01, wait no, it does! level1 extends base01\n5. Since base01 doesn't have ExtendsNode, we add base01.blocks = {first: base01's first, second: base01's second}6. Renders base01OK so blocks after all adds:- blocks[inner] = [base01's inner, level1's inner, level2's inner]  (after all inserts at 0)But wait, base01 doesn't define inner! So blocks[inner] wouldn't be updated with base01's inner.\n\nLet me retrace:1. level2.blocks added: blocks[inner] = [level2's inner]\n2. level1.blocks added:   - level1.blocks = {first: level1's first, inner: level1's inner}   - add_blocks({first: level1's first, inner: level1's inner})   - blocks[first] = [level1's first]   - blocks[inner] = [level1's inner, level2's inner]  (insert at 0)3. base01.blocks added:   - base01.blocks = {first: base01's first, second: base01's second}   - add_blocks({first: base01's first, second: base01's second})\n   - blocks[first] = [base01's first, level1's first]   - blocks[second] = [base01's second]   - blocks[inner] remains [level1's inner, level2's inner] (no inner in base01.blocks)\n\nWhen rendering base01, the first block pops level1's first, then renders its nodelist which includes level1's inner block. When that inner block renders, it pops level2's inner from the stack, leaving level1's inner still there. This is where the leak happens—level1's inner content gets rendered when it shouldn't be.\n\nThe core issue seems to be a mismatch in how blocks are pushed versus popped during nested rendering. When level1's first finishes rendering, level1's inner remains on the stack even though it was already consumed by level2's inner. This creates a situation where the same block content could be rendered multiple times or in the wrong context. pping from the block_context, rendering the nodelist, and then pushing back. But if that nodelist contains other BlockNodes, those nested blocks might also pop and push, creating an unbalanced sequence that leaves the wrong blocks in the stack.\n\nWhen popping \"inner\" and retrieving level2's version, I create a new BlockNode with its name and nodelist, render it, then push the original back. If level2's inner contained nested blocks, they'd trigger their own pop/push operations during rendering. However, in this case there aren't any nested blocks within level2's inner, so that's not the culprit here.\n\nPerhaps the real problem lies elsewhere—maybe the blocks are being collected in an unexpected order due to how the dictionary comprehension processes them, or the get_nodes_by_type extraction follows a depth-first or breadth-first traversal that doesn't match what I'm assuming. {"type":"assistant","message":{"model":"claude-haiku-4-5-20251001","id":"msg_016fuYebwvnZoEK2g16Apcg5","type":"message","role":"assistant","content":[{"type":"thinking","thinking":"I see! The issue is that we add THIS template's blocks (line 142) BEFORE checking if the parent has blocks and adding those. But if the parent has an ExtendsNode, we don't add the parent's blocks immediately. Instead, we render the parent, which will trigger the parent's ExtendsNode.render(), which will then add the parent's blocks.\n\nSo the order of add_blocks calls is:\n1. leaf's blocks added\n2. leaf renders mid.html\n3. mid's blocks added\n4. mid renders base.html (after adding base's blocks)\n5. base.html renders\n\nActually, I think the problem might be different. What if the issue is that when the PARENT template has an ExtendsNode, we're NOT adding the parent's blocks in the right place?\n\nLooking at the code, if the parent has an ExtendsNode (line 149: `if not isinstance(node, ExtendsNode)`), we DON'T add the parent's blocks. Instead, we rely on the parent's ExtendsNode.render() to add them when the parent is rendered.\n\nSo the flow is:\n1. leaf.ExtendsNode.render() adds leaf.blocks\n2. leaf.ExtendsNode.render() doesn't add mid.blocks (because mid has ExtendsNode)\n3. leaf.ExtendsNode.render() renders mid.html\n4. mid.ExtendsNode.render() adds mid.blocks\n5. mid.ExtendsNode.render() does add base.blocks (because base doesn't have ExtendsNode)\n6. mid.ExtendsNode.render() renders base.html\n\nSo the order is: leaf.blocks, mid.blocks, base.blocks. That's the order I expected.\n\nBut wait! What if there's a subtle issue with WHEN the parent's blocks are added? In step 5, we add base.blocks BEFORE rendering base.html. So base.blocks are added to the block_context before base.html starts rendering.\n\n\nMaybe the blocks are being extracted correctly, but there's an issue with how they're being used. Perhaps I should try a different approach where nested blocks inside other blocks we're defining don't get extracted separately.","signature":""}],"stop_reason":null,"stop_sequence":null,"usage":{"input_tokens":0,"cache_creation_input_tokens":972,"cache_read_input_tokens":104686,"cache_creation":{"ephemeral_5m_input_tokens":0,"ephemeral_1h_input_tokens":972},"output_tokens":8,"service_tier":"standard","inference_geo":"not_available"},"context_management":null},"parent_tool_use_id":null,"session_id":"9289f73d-547f-4b33-bcf6-616cce479223","uuid":"7349910a-4633-4b49-9fe9-c813eebc4a31"}

Let me re-read the BlockNode.render() code with fresh eyes:\n\n```python\ndef render(self, context):\n    block_context = context.render_context.get(BLOCK_CONTEXT_KEY)\n    with context.push():\n        if block_context is None:\n            context[\"block\"] = self\n            result = self.nodelist.render(context)\n        else:\n            push = block = block_context.pop(self.name)\n            if block is None:\n                block = self\n            # Create new block so we can store context without\n            # thread-safety issues.\n            block = type(self)(block.name, block.nodelist)\n            block.context = context\n            context[\"block\"] = block\n            result = block.nodelist.render(context)\n            if push is not None:\n                block_context.push(self.name, push)\n    return result\n```\n\nWait, I see an issue! When we pop a block and render it, we're rendering the POPPED BLOCK's nodelist, not self's nodelist!\n\nBut wait, that's correct. We pop an override from the block_context, which should be a child template's block, and render its nodelist. That's the intended behavior.\n\nHmm, actually wait. Let me reconsider once more. When we have:\n- base.html's outer: nodelist = [TextNode(\"BASE[\"), base.html's inner, TextNode(\"]\")]\n- mid.html's outer: nodelist = [TextNode(\"MID[\"), mid.html's inner, TextNode(\"]\")]\n\n\n- leaf.html overrides inner: nodelist = [TextNode(\"OUT\")]\n\nWhen we pop(\"outer\") and get mid.html's outer, we render mid.html's outer's nodelist. This nodelist contains mid.html's inner BlockNode object! When mid.html's inner BlockNode is rendered, it calls BlockNode.render(), which pops from block_context. At this point, we should have blocks[inner] = [base's inner, mid's inner, leaf's inner], and pop() should return leaf's inner. So everything should be correct... UNLESS! What if the issue is with dictionary ordering in Python 3.6 vs 3.7+? Or what if get_nodes_by_type returns blocks in a different order than expected?\n\nMaybe get_nodes_by_type is actually returning blocks in reverse order from what I'm assuming. I should write a test to verify the actual order being returned.","signature":""}],"stop_reason":null,"stop_sequence":null,"usage":{"input_tokens":0,"cache_creation_input_tokens":994,"cache_read_input_tokens":107187,"cache_creation":{"ephemeral_5m_input_tokens":0,"ephemeral_1h_input_tokens":994},"output_tokens":3,"service_tier":"standard","inference_geo":"not_available"},"context_management":null},"parent_tool_use_id":null,"session_id":"9289f73d-547f-4b33-bcf6-616cce479223","uuid":"77a75ae5-7f33-4dbb-8000-b477ae23ac6c"}

Dictionary of block stacks - blocks are stored with parent first, child last\n        self.blocks = defaultdict(list)\n\n    def __repr__(self):\n        return f\"<{self.__class__.__qualname__}: blocks={self.blocks!r}>\"\n\n    def add_blocks(self, blocks):\n        for name, block in blocks.items():\n            self.blocks[name].insert(0, block)\n\n    def pop(self, name):\n        try:\n            return self.blocks[name].pop()\n        except IndexError:\n            return None\n\n    def push(self, name, block):\n        self.blocks[name].append(block)\n\n    def get_block(self, name):\n        try:\n            return self.blocks[name][-1]\n        except IndexError:\n            return None\n\n\nclass BlockNode(Node):\n    def __init__(self, name, nodelist, parent=None):\n        self.name = name\n        self.nodelist = nodelist\n        self.parent = parent\n\n    def __repr__(self):\n        return \"<Block Node: %s. A history is kept in\n        the render_context attribute between successive extends calls and\n        passed as the skip argument. This enables extends to work recursively\n        without extending the same template twice.\n        \"\"\"\n        history = context.render_context.setdefault(\n            self.context_key,\n            [self.origin],\n        )\n        template, origin = context.template.engine.find_template(\n            template_name,\n            skip=history,\n        )\n        history.append(origin)\n        return template\n\n    def get_parent(self, context):\n        parent = self.parent_name.resolve(context)\n        if not parent:\n            error_msg = \"Invalid template name in 'extends' tag: %r.\" % parent\n            if self.parent_name.filters or isinstance(self.parent_name.var, Variable):\n                error_msg += (\n                    \" Got this from the '%s' variable.\" % self.parent_name.token\n                )\n            raise TemplateSyntaxError(error_msg)\n        if isinstance(parent, Template):\n            # Add the block nodes from this node to the block context\n        block_context.add_blocks(self.blocks)\n\n        # If this block's parent doesn't have an extends node it is the root,\n        # and its block nodes also need to be added to the block context.\n        for node in compiled_parent.nodelist:\n            # The ExtendsNode has to be the first non-text node.\n            if not isinstance(node, TextNode):\n                if not isinstance(node, ExtendsNode):\n                    blocks = {\n                        n.name: n\n                        for n in compiled_parent.nodelist.get_nodes_by_type(BlockNode)\n                    }\n                    block_context.add_blocks(blocks)\n                break\n\n        # Call Template._render explicitly so the parser context stays\n        # the same.\n        with context.render_context.push_state(compiled_parent, isolated_context=False):\n            return compiled_parent._render(context)\n\n\nclass IncludeNode(Node):\n    context_key = \"__include_context\"\n\n    def __init__(\n        self, template, *args, extra_context=None, isolated_context=False, **kwargs\n    ):\n        self.template = template\n        self.extra_context = extra_context or {}\n        self.isolated_context = isolated_context\n        super().__init__(*args, **kwargs)\n\n    def __repr__(self):\n        return f\"<{self.__class__.__qualname__}: template={self.template!r}>\"\n\n    def render(self, context):\n        \"\"\"\n        Render the specified template and context. Cache the template object\n        in render_context to avoid reparsing and loading when used in a for\n        loop.\n        \"\"\"\n        template = self.template.resolve(context)\n        # Does this quack like a Template?\n        if not callable(getattr(template, \"render\", None)):\n            # token.split_contents() isn't useful here because this tag doesn't accept\n    # variable as arguments.\n    bits = token.contents.split()\n    if len(bits) != 2:\n        raise TemplateSyntaxError(\"'%s' tag takes only one argument\" % bits[0])\n    block_name = bits[1]\n    #    allow_recursion=False,\n):\n    \"\"\"\n    Convert a relative path (starting with './' or '../') to the full template\n    name based on the current_template_name.\n    \"\"\"\n    new_name = relative_name.strip(\"'\\\"\")\n    if not new_name.startswith((\"./\", \"../\")):\n        # relative_name is a variable or a literal that doesn't contain a\n        # relative path.\n        return path '%s' points outside the file hierarchy that \"\n            \"template '%s' is in.\" % (relative_name, current_template_name)\n        )\n    if not allow_recursion and current_template_name.lstrip(\"/\") == new_name:\n        raise TemplateSyntaxError(\n            \"The relative path '%s' was translated to template name '%s', the \"\n            \"same template in which the tag appears.\"\n            % (relative_name, current_template_name)\n        )\n    has_quotes = (\n        relative_name.startswith(('\"', \"'\")) and relative_name[0] == relative_name[-1]\n    )\n    return f'\"{new_name}\"' if has_quotes else new_name\n\n\n@register.tag(\"extends\")\ndef do_extends(parser, token):\n    \"\"\"\n    Signal that this template extends a parent template.\n\n    This tag may be used in two ways: ``{% extends \"base\" %}`` (with quotes)\n    uses the literal value \"base\" as the name of the parent template to extend,\n    or ``{% extends variable %}`` uses the value of ``variable`` as either the\n    name of the parent template to extend (if it evaluates to a string) or as\n    the parent template itself (if it evaluates to a raise TemplateSyntaxError(\n            \"'%s' cannot appear more than once in the same template\" % bits[0]\n        )\n    return ExtendsNode(nodelist, parent_name)\n\n\n@register.tag(\"include\")\ndef do_include(parser, token):\n    \"\"\"\n    Load a template and render it with the current context. You can pass\n    additional context using keyword arguments.\n\n    Example::\n\n        {% include \"foo/some_include\" %}\n        {% include \"foo/some_include\" with bar=\"BAZZ!\" baz=\"BING!\" %}\n\n    Use the ``only`` argument to exclude the current context when rendering\n    the included template::\n\n        {% include \"foo/some_include\" only %}\n        {% include \"foo/some_include\" with bar=\"1\" only %}\n    \"\"\"\n    bits = token.split_contents()\n    if len(bits) < 2:\n        raise TemplateSyntaxError(\n            \"%r tag takes at least one argument: the name of the template to \"\n            \"be included.\" % bits[0]\n        )\n    options = {}\n    remaining_bits = bits[2:]\n    while remaining_bits:\n        option = remaining_bits.pop(0)\n        if option in options:\n            raise TemplateSyntaxError(\n                \"The %r option was specified more than once.\" % option\n            )\n        if option == \"with\":\n extends variable %}`` uses the value of ``variable`` as either the\n    name of the parent template to extend (if it evaluates to a string) or as\n    the parent template itself (if it evaluates to a Let me re-read the ExtendsNode.render() code and focus on the push_state call:\n\n```python\nwith context.render_context.push_state(compiled_parent, isolated_context=False):    return compiled_parent._render(context)\n```\n\nWe're passing `isolated_context=False`, which means the render_context is NOT isolated. So the block_context is shared across the rendering of all templates!\n\nNow, when we render multiple levels of templates, we're modifying the same block_context by popping and pushing. If the pop/push balance is wrong, we might end up with blocks in the wrong state!\n\nLet me think about when push and pop are called...When rendering leaf.html:\n1. leaf.ExtendsNode renders mid.html\n2. mid.html's template._render() renders mid.html's nodes (which starts with mid.ExtendsNode)\n\n\n3. mid.ExtendsNode renders base.html\n4. base.html's template._render() renders base.html's nodes (which starts with base.html's blocks)\n5. base.html's BlockNode(\"outer\") calls render(), which pops and pushes\n6. This modifies the block_context that's shared with leaf and mid!\n\nSo if the pop/push isn't balanced correctly across all levels, we might end up with blocks in the wrong state. I think the issue is in BlockNode.render() - when it pops a block from the context, it's pulling from the shared block_context, and if there are multiple levels, the pop/push sequence might be leaving blocks in an inconsistent state.\n\nThe problem becomes clear when nested blocks are involved: if an outer block pops its value and then renders its nodelist, any inner blocks within that nodelist will also pop and push. This interleaving of operations could corrupt the stack order, especially when the inner block's push happens before the outer block's push completes.\n\nConsider the sequence: rendering base.html's outer block pops \"outer\" to get mid's version, then during the rendering of mid's nodelist, an inner block pops \"inner\" to get leaf's version and pushes it back. Only after that does the outer block push back. The real issue is that we're instantiating a fresh BlockNode with the popped nodelist, so the object being pushed back might not match what was originally popped, breaking the symmetry of the stack operations.\n\nLooking at the actual implementation though, we push back the popped block itself rather than the newly created one, so that's not the culprit. {"type":"assistant","message":{"model":"claude-haiku-4-5-20251001","id":"msg_01U1mmkUGm9wW1xP9v7J9zBA","type":"message","role":"assistant","content":[{"type":"thinking","thinking":"The code looks correct. The block context methods are implemented properly:- add_blocks inserts at position 0\n- pop removes from the end- push appends to the end\n\nThis creates a proper LIFO stack structure where:- insert(0, x) adds x to the front, shifting others right\n- pop() removes from the end (the oldest item in the LIFO sense)Actually wait, let me double-check that semantics...If we have a list [A, B, C] and we pop(), we get C (the last item). Then the list is [A, B].\n\nIf we start with [] and do:\n1. insert(0, X): [X]\n2. insert(0, Y): [Y, X]  (Y is inserted at 0, pushes X to index 1)\n3. insert(0, Z): [Z, Y, X]  (Z is inserted at 0, pushes Y and X right)\n\nThen pop() gives us X, not Z. So we get the FIRST item inserted (in terms of LIFO logic where the most recently added is at the front but pops from the back give us the old items first).\n\nActually, this is confusing. Let me think about it differently:- insert(0, block) adds block to position 0\n- pop() removes from position -1 (the last position)So with insert(0) adding to the front, and pop() removing from the back:- After insert(0, A): [A]\n- After insert(0, B): [B, A]  (B is now at index 0, A is at index 1)\n- After insert(0, C): [C, B, A]  (C is now at index 0)\n- pop() returns A (index 2, which is -1)So pop() returns A, which was the FIRST item inserted. This is FIFO behavior (First In, First Out), not LIFO!\n\nHmm, but that's not right either. \n\nTracing through the actual block additions: leaf gets inserted at position 0, then mid at position 0 (shifting leaf right), then base at position 0 (shifting mid and leaf right). This gives [base_inner, mid_inner, leaf_inner]. When we pop(), we remove from index -1, which is leaf_inner—the most recently added block. That's the correct LIFO behavior. The semantics work out correctly—insert(0, block) adds to the front while pop() removes from the back, ensuring the most recent override is always returned first. Regardless of whether this maps to traditional stack terminology, the behavior is what matters, and it delivers the right result.","signature":""}],"stop_reason":null,"stop_sequence":null,"usage":{"input_tokens":0,"cache_creation_input_tokens":649,"cache_read_input_tokens":123053,"cache_creation":{"ephemeral_5m_input_tokens":0,"ephemeral_1h_input_tokens":649},"output_tokens":3,"service_tier":"standard","inference_geo":"not_available"},"context_management":null},"parent_tool_use_id":null,"session_id":"9289f73d-547f-4b33-bcf6-616cce479223","uuid":"dd38b57d-9538-4aab-b892-9c0d92e38cf7"}
